figures.caption: Fig. 2. A programmatic policy discovered by ProRL. Depending on the state, the policy will choose an action from three heuristics: MWR, LOR and SPT. The concepts {LD,AM,AO, JD, ST} represent machine load balance, available machine ratio, available operation ratio, job remaining time balance, and shortest operation remaining time balance, respectively.

Table 1. Results (gaps to BKS) grouped by benchmarks [31, 6]. The best results (including PDRs and PPOPDR) are bold. PPOPDR learns to select PDRs with a neural network. The “random” agent randomly chooses PDRs.
Fig. 4. Visualization of the policy shown in Fig. 2 via t-SNE. Three PDRs (MWR, LOR and SPT) are clustered well.
Fig. 3. ProRL locally searches for architectures and then optimizes the program parameters via BO. The best program is mutated for generate the new neighborhood.

Table 5. Additional results grouped by benchmarks ABZ [1], LA [12], SWV [19], FT [8], ORB [2] and YN [29]. The best results (including PDR heuristics and PPO) are bold. PPO learns to select PDRs with a neural network. “Random” randomly chooses PDRs. ProRL outperforms PDRs and PPOPDR.
Table 7. Average training time in seconds.
Table 6. Additional results of the low-budget performance validation. ProRL outperforms PDRs and PPOPDR, even with a budget of only 100 episodes.
Table 4. Comparison with reported results of neural methods including L2D [40], L2S [39], GM [5] and SI GD [23]. The best PDR per instance is denoted as “mPDR”.
Table 2. Results of the low-budget performance validation on benchmarks [31, 6]. ProRL outperforms PDRs and PPOPDR, even with only 100 episode budget.
Table 8. Comparison to reported results. Results of DQN [11] and PPOPDR [28] are directly obtained from their published articles. ProRL outperforms PDRs and DRL baselines.
Table 9. List of licenses for asserts used in this work
